metadata.career_relevance: I/O code — file readers, CSV parsers, API clients, log writers — is in every real application. Testing it without a real filesystem or network requires the right strategies. @TempDir gives you a real filesystem sandbox that auto-cleans. Mocking HttpClient lets you test API clients without a server. After this topic, every class from Module 11 (NIO files, BufferedReader, HttpClient) has a proper test suite.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll use @TempDir for file-based tests, write test data programmatically, assert on file contents, mock HttpClient and HttpResponse to test API clients without a server, and use StringReader/StringWriter to test I/O code without files.

visual_aid.description: Three panels: (1) @TempDir lifecycle — timeline showing: JUnit creates temp dir → @BeforeEach → @Test runs (writes/reads files inside tempDir) → @AfterEach → JUnit deletes temp dir (even if test failed). Contrast with manual approach: files left on disk if test throws. (2) HttpClient mock setup: HttpClient (mock) → returns HttpResponse (mock) → response.statusCode() = 200, response.body() = JSON string → ApiClient parses with Jackson. Arrow from 'when(client.send(...))' to stubbed response. (3) StringReader/StringWriter as I/O surrogates: String → StringReader → BufferedReader → class under test vs class under test → PrintWriter → StringWriter → String. Both avoid filesystem entirely.
visual_aid.alt_text: @TempDir auto-cleanup lifecycle, mocked HttpClient chain, StringReader/Writer as file surrogates

worked_example.scenario: Write a complete test suite for an `InventoryReportService` that reads a CSV file, processes it, and writes a report. Tests cover reading, writing, error paths, and edge cases — all using @TempDir and StringReader/Writer.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Test `InventoryReportService` with:
1. Valid CSV → correct report file created
2. Empty CSV → empty report
3. CSV with malformed rows → valid rows processed, bad rows skipped
4. Write report to @TempDir and assert its contents
5. Non-existent input file → NoSuchFileException
6. Report format: header line + one line per product
worked_example.expected_output:
Test Results:
✓ readProducts_validCsv_returns3Products
✓ readProducts_emptyFile_returnsEmptyList
✓ readProducts_malformedRow_skipsAndContinues
✓ readProducts_nonExistentFile_throwsNoSuchFileException
✓ writeReport_3products_correctContent
✓ fullPipeline_readThenWrite_roundTrip

6 tests passed, 0 failed
things_to_notice: @TempDir is injected as a method parameter (`@TempDir Path tempDir`). JUnit creates the directory before the test and deletes everything in it after the test — even if the test fails. No manual cleanup ever needed.

Files.writeString(tempDir.resolve('name.csv'), content) — combine tempDir with resolve() to get a path inside the sandbox. Never hardcode paths like '/tmp/test.csv' — that causes test collisions and leaves files on disk.

The empty file test writes only a header line. The service skips the header (skip(1)) and finds no data rows — returning an empty list. This tests the boundary between 'file has data' and 'file has no data'.

The malformed row test verifies the service gracefully skips bad rows (caught by the try-catch returning null, then filtered out) while still processing valid rows. This is a common real-world requirement.

The full pipeline test (read → write → verify) is an integration test within the unit test suite — it tests the two methods together. This catches bugs that individual tests might miss (e.g., the writer doesn't write what the reader parsed).

Files.readAllLines(report) after writing lets you assert line-by-line. Files.readString(report) lets you assert on the full content with contains(). Both are appropriate — choose based on what you're checking.

industry_examples.use_case: The inventory import service reads seller CSV uploads from S3 (or local file). Tests use @TempDir to simulate the file system: write a test CSV, run the importer, assert on the result. Error path tests: write a CSV with bad rows, verify error report is written to @TempDir. No real S3 needed for unit tests — just the file parsing logic.

Spring Boot's @WebMvcTest slice mocks the service layer and tests only the HTTP controller layer. For REST APIs that return file downloads, tests use MockMvc (similar to mocking HttpClient) to send GET requests and assert on the response body and headers — without starting a real server.

Log parser tests write sample log files to @TempDir (apache-style, JSON, or custom format), run the parser, and assert on the extracted error counts, timestamps, and endpoints. Testing error paths: write a log file with a corrupted JSON line, verify the parser skips it. @TempDir makes this simple and clean.

applications.interview_relevance:
I/O testing questions:

- **'How do you test a method that reads from a file?'** → Write a test file to @TempDir, pass the path to the method, assert on the result.
- **'How do you test a method that writes a file?'** → Pass a path in @TempDir to the method, then read the file back and assert its content.
- **'How do you test an HTTP client without a real server?'** → Mock HttpClient with @Mock, stub client.send() to return a mock HttpResponse, stub response.statusCode() and response.body().
- **'What is @TempDir in JUnit 5?'** → A JUnit 5 annotation that creates a temporary directory for use in tests. It's automatically deleted after the test — even if it fails.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily I/O testing patterns:

- File reader: `@TempDir Path dir; Files.writeString(dir.resolve('data.csv'), testData); List<T> result = reader.read(csvPath)`
- File writer: `service.write(dir.resolve('out.txt'), data); assertEquals(expected, Files.readString(outPath))`
- Stream/Reader: `new BufferedReader(new StringReader(testData))` — no file needed
- HTTP client: `@Mock HttpClient client; when(client.send(any(), any())).thenReturn(mockResponse)`
- Error paths: nonexistent path, empty file, malformed content, network timeout

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using hardcoded paths like `/tmp/test.csv` or `test.csv` (relative) instead of @TempDir.
common_gaps.remediation: Always use @TempDir for file-based tests. Hardcoded paths cause: test collisions when tests run in parallel, leftover files when tests fail, failures on machines where /tmp doesn't exist. `@TempDir Path tempDir; tempDir.resolve('file.csv')` is always correct.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Tests pass locally but fail on CI; file cleanup errors in test output

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Asserting on file content without first checking the file was created.
common_gaps.remediation: Before asserting content, always assert the file exists: `assertTrue(Files.exists(reportPath), 'Report file should be created')`. Otherwise a missing file gives a confusing NoSuchFileException instead of a clear 'file not found' assertion failure.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: NoSuchFileException from Files.readAllLines() with unclear error message

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Mocking HttpResponse without type parameter: `@Mock HttpResponse response` instead of `@Mock HttpResponse<String> response` — type warnings and potential mismatches.
common_gaps.remediation: Always parameterize: `@Mock HttpResponse<String> response`. The send() method is typed as `send(HttpRequest, BodyHandler<T>) → HttpResponse<T>`. Matching the type parameter avoids unchecked warnings and type errors.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Unchecked cast warnings; type mismatch compilation errors

key_takeaways: @TempDir Path tempDir: JUnit creates real temp directory, auto-deleted after test
tempDir.resolve('filename.csv'): create path inside temp dir
Files.writeString(path, content): write test data; Files.readAllLines(path): read output
StringReader: test BufferedReader-accepting methods without a file
StringWriter: test PrintWriter-accepting methods, capture output as String
@Mock HttpResponse<String> response: typed mock for HttpResponse
Test all HTTP status scenarios: 200, 404, 500, and IOException

self_check_questions.question: You need to test that `LogWriter.writeLine(Path file, String line)` appends a line to an existing file. How do you set this up with @TempDir?
Your ApiClient has a `private static final HttpClient HTTP = HttpClient.newHttpClient()`. Can you mock it with @Mock?
self_check_questions.answer: No — static final fields can't be injected by Mockito. You need to make the HttpClient injectable: either pass it via constructor (`ApiClient(HttpClient client)`) or via a setter. @InjectMocks only works when Mockito can inject via constructor, setter, or field. Redesign the class to accept HttpClient as a dependency rather than creating it internally. This is the dependency injection principle — and it makes the code testable.

self_check_questions.question: What is the benefit of testing file parsing with StringReader instead of @TempDir?
self_check_questions.answer: StringReader is faster (no filesystem), simpler (no @TempDir setup), and works at the Reader/InputStream level. Use StringReader when the method accepts a Reader/InputStream — you don't need to create a file at all. Use @TempDir when the method accepts a Path/File directly. If you can refactor the method to accept a Reader instead of Path, testing becomes even simpler. Many file parsing methods follow this pattern: one method takes a Path and creates a reader, another takes the reader directly — test the reader version with StringReader.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 12 topic: **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** — write the test FIRST, then write the code to make it pass. Red (test fails — no code yet) → Green (write minimum code to pass) → Refactor (improve code, tests still pass). After Topic 12.5, you have a complete testing toolkit: JUnit 5, parameterized tests, Mockito, I/O testing, and TDD. Module 12 is the final foundational module before the course moves into specialized topics.
